questions.prompt: What was the core lesson of Tesla's 'production hell' during the Model 3 ramp?

choices.text: Don't automate tasks where humans have real advantages. Robots excel at high-force, precision, repetition, hazardous work. Humans excel at flexibility, variable tolerances, soft materials, complex 3D paths, and judgment. Tesla automated tasks in the second category (wire harness routing, foam placement, intricate sub-assembly) and the line broke. Match task to tool; hybrid lines beat all-automated or all-manual.

questions.explanation: The 2017-2018 hell was over-automation of tasks humans do better. The fix was retreating to humans for flexibility-demanding work while keeping robots where they win. Musk publicly admitted the mistake. The deeper lesson: match automation to task characteristics, not to ambition.

questions.prompt: A startup is designing their first manufacturing line. Should they automate everything 'like Tesla originally tried' or run mostly manual?

choices.text: Full automation from day one — modern factories must be.

Start with humans, automate from there. Humans build the V1 line and reveal which tasks are repetitive, high-precision, dangerous, or rigid — automate those over time. Tasks that remain variable, judgment-requiring, or soft-material-handling stay with humans. This is the TPS-style approach Tesla learned the hard way in 2018. Faster to launch; lower risk; better outcomes.

questions.explanation: Start manual; automate selectively. Humans on V1 lines reveal task characteristics and edge cases. Automate the parts where robots clearly win; leave humans where they outperform. Faster, cheaper, more reliable than trying to automate before you understand the process.
